title: Hawawshi
subtitle: Egyptian spiced minced meat in baked bread
excerpt: Spiced minced beef mixed with onion, green pepper, parsley, and cumin, stuffed into pitta bread, and baked until the bread is crisp and the filling is cooked through. Egyptian street food eaten at any hour. The minced meat seasons and flavours the bread from the inside as they bake together.
sourceNotes: Hawawshi is one of the most popular Egyptian street foods, associated with Cairo's working-class neighbourhoods and butcher shops where the filling was traditionally made fresh each morning. It is named after a 19th-century butcher called Hawawsh who reportedly invented it. Variations include spicy (with chilli), onion-heavy, and cheese versions.

content.text: The pitta bread must be opened into a pocket without tearing, slide a knife along one edge to open it. Stuff generously: the filling should fill the bread completely so every bite is full. Press the stuffed pitta flat with your hand before baking to ensure even cooking.

Use a rack or wire oven tray rather than a flat baking sheet: the hot air circulating underneath crisps the bottom of the bread. Without the rack, the underside steams and stays soft. Bake until the outside is deeply golden and the filling is fully cooked, about 20 to 25 minutes at 200°C.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Mix the {{beef-mince}}, {{onion}}, {{pepper-green}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} together until fully combined. Open each {{pitta-bread}} along one edge to create a pocket. Divide the filling between the pittas, pressing it in firmly to fill the whole bread evenly. Press the stuffed pitta flat with your hand. Brush the outside with {{vegetable-oil}}. Place on a wire rack over a baking tray. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the bread is golden and crisp and the filling is cooked through. Cut into wedges and serve with a squeeze of lemon and hot sauce.

items.fix: Use a wire rack so air circulates under the bread. Ensure the oven is fully preheated to 200°C before the hawawshi goes in
items.cause: Placed on a flat sheet rather than a rack, or oven not hot enough

items.fix: Keep the filling layer to 1.5 cm thick maximum. Press the pitta flat after stuffing so the filling is evenly distributed
items.cause: Filling too thick, bread stuffed too generously
items.symptom: Filling still raw in the centre

items.fix: Wrap the pitta in a damp cloth for 2 minutes to soften slightly before opening. Slide a butter knife along the seam rather than using fingers

content.text: Hawawshi is Cairo street food, sold from bakeries and dedicated shops where the stuffed pitta goes directly into a wood-fired oven. It is eaten for breakfast, as a mid-morning snack, or as a late-night meal. The Cairo neighbourhood of Rod el-Farag is particularly associated with it. In Egyptian homes it is made for quick weeknight suppers, and the recipe varies by family: some add tomato, some add chilli, some add cheese. It travels well and stays crisp for 30 minutes after baking, which makes it well suited to packed lunches.
